en.extract:
The speckle-breasted antpitta (Cryptopezus nattereri) is a species of bird in the family Grallariidae. It is found in Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay.


Taxonomy and systematics
The speckle-breasted antpitta has a complicated taxonomic history. It was originally described in 1937 as Grallaria natteri. Soon it was realized that it and several other species did not belong in Grallaria so genus Hylopezus, which had earlier been merged into Grallaria, was resurrected for them. By the middle of the century the speckle-breasted antpitta had been reallocated as a subspecies of the white-browed antpitta (Hylopezus ochroleucus). A 1995 publication showed that it should again be treated as the species Hylopezus natteri. A 2019 publication showed that Hylopezus was paraphyletic with respect to two other genera and that the speckle-breasted antpitta properly belonged to none of them. The authors erected genus Cryptopezus for it.
The speckle-breasted antpitta is the only member of its genus and has no subspecies.


Description
The speckle-breasted antpitta is about 13.5 to 14 cm (5.3 to 5.5 in) long; two males weighed 31 and 33 g (1.1 and 1.2 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have pale buff lores, a whitish "moustache", and a ring of bare buffy white skin around their eye. Their crown, nape, and upperparts are olive-brown. Their wings are browner than their back, with dark primary coverts that also have a small ochraceous spot. Their throat and the center of their belly are white. Their breast, sides and flanks are pale ochraceous-buff with a darker scallop pattern on the sides and dusky spots on the breast, upper belly, and flanks. They have a dark brown iris, a blackish bill with a pinkish gray base to the mandible, and pinkish gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The speckle-breasted antpitta is found in Brazil from southern Minas Gerais and western Rio de Janeiro state south to northern Rio Grande do Sul and west into extreme eastern Paraguay and northeastern Argentina's Misiones Province. It inhabits the floor and undergrowth in the interior and edges of humid montane forest and mature secondary woodland. It especially favors areas with dense tangled vegetation and bamboo. In elevation it ranges between 1,200 and 1,900 m (3,900 and 6,200 ft) in the northern part of its range and to below 300 m (1,000 ft) in the south.


Behavior


Movement
The speckle-breasted antpitta is believed to be resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The speckle-breasted antpitta's diet is not known. It is primarily terrestrial, and forages with short hops and abrupt stops to study and probe leave litter and soil.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the speckle-breasted antpitta's breeding biology beyond that a bird was seen in September on a nest made of sticks.


Vocalization
The speckle-breasted antpitta's song is a "short, fairly fast beautiful, fluted series of about 10 notes increasing in volume and pitch". It typically sings from a perch up to about 2 m (7 ft) above the ground. Its call is "a 1-second rattle...of 7 short, sharply downslurred notes".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the speckle-breasted antpitta as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common in most of its range and occurs in several protected areas.


References

The '''speckle-breasted antpitta''' ('''''Cryptopezus nattereri''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Grallariidae]]. It is found in [[Argentina]], [[Brazil]], and [[Paraguay]].<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/antthrushes/ |title=Antthrushes, antpittas, gnateaters, tapaculos, crescentchests |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The speckle-breasted antpitta has a complicated taxonomic history. It was originally [[Species description|described]] in 1937 as ''Grallaria natteri''.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Pinto |first=Olivério  |date=1937 |title=Grallaria natteri sp. nov |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48341643 |journal=Boletim biológico |volume=III |issue=7 |page=7 |language=Portuguese |access-date=September 10, 2024 }}</ref> Soon it was realized that it and several other species did not belong in ''Grallaria'' so genus ''Hylopezus'', which had earlier been merged into ''Grallaria'', was resurrected for them. By the middle of the century the speckle-breasted antpitta had been reallocated as a subspecies of the [[white-browed antpitta]] (''Hylopezus ochroleucus'').<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 27 July 2024. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved July 28, 2024</ref> A 1995 publication showed that it should again be treated as the species ''Hylopezus natteri''.<ref>Whitney, B. M., J. F. Pacheco, P. R. Isler, and M. L. Isler (1995). ''Hylopezus nattereri'' (Pinto, 1937) is a valid species (Passeriformes: Formicariidae). Ararajuba 3:37–42.</ref> A 2019 publication showed that ''Hylopezus'' was [[paraphyletic]] with respect to two other genera and that the speckle-breasted antpitta properly belonged to none of them. The authors erected genus ''Cryptopezus'' for it.<ref>Carneiro, L.S., G. A. Bravo, and A. Aleixo. 2019. Phenotypic similarity leads to taxonomic inconsistency: A revision of the lowland's antpittas. Zoologica Scripta 48:46–56</ref>

The speckle-breasted antpitta is the only member of its genus and has no subspecies.<ref name=IOC14.2/>

==Description==

The speckle-breasted antpitta is about {{convert|13.5|to|14|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long; two males weighed {{convert|31|and|33|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have pale buff [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]], a whitish "moustache", and a ring of bare buffy white skin around their eye. Their crown, nape, and upperparts are olive-brown. Their wings are browner than their back, with dark [[Primaries (birds)|primary]] [[coverts]] that also have a small ochraceous spot. Their throat and the center of their belly are white. Their breast, sides and flanks are pale ochraceous-buff with a darker scallop pattern on the sides and dusky spots on the breast, upper belly, and flanks. They have a dark brown iris, a blackish bill with a pinkish gray base to the [[mandible]], and pinkish gray legs and feet.<ref name=SBAN-BOW>Krabbe, N. and T. S. Schulenberg (2021). Speckle-breasted Antpitta (''Cryptopezus nattereri''), version 1.1. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.spbant1.01.1 retrieved September 10, 2024</ref><ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =266–267 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref><ref name=Peña>{{cite book | last =de la Peña | first =Martín R. | last2=Rumboll |first2=Maurice|  title =Birds of Southern South America and Antarctica | publisher =Princeton University Press | series =Princeton Illustrated Checklists | date =2001 | location =New Jersey | pages =Plate 68, map 68.10 | isbn =0-691-09035-1 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The speckle-breasted antpitta is found in Brazil from southern [[Minas Gerais]] and western [[Rio de Janeiro (state)|Rio de Janeiro state]] south to northern [[Rio Grande do Sul]] and west into extreme eastern Paraguay and northeastern Argentina's [[Misiones Province]]. It inhabits the floor and undergrowth in the interior and edges of humid [[montane forest]] and mature [[secondary woodland]]. It especially favors areas with dense tangled vegetation and bamboo. In elevation it ranges between {{convert|1200|and|1900|m|ft|abbr=on}} in the northern part of its range and to below {{convert|300|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} in the south.<ref name=SBAN-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Peña/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The speckle-breasted antpitta is believed to be resident throughout its range.<ref name=SBAN-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The speckle-breasted antpitta's diet is not known. It is primarily terrestrial, and forages with short hops and abrupt stops to study and probe leave litter and soil.<ref name=SBAN-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the speckle-breasted antpitta's breeding biology beyond that a bird was seen in September on a nest made of sticks.<ref name=SBAN-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Cryptopezus-nattereri  |species=the speckle-breasted antpitta}}
===Vocalization===

The speckle-breasted antpitta's song is a "short, fairly fast beautiful, fluted series of about 10 notes increasing in volume and pitch".<ref name=vanPerlo/> It typically sings from a perch up to about {{convert|2|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground. Its call is "a 1-second rattle...of 7 short, sharply downslurred notes".<ref name=SBAN-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the speckle-breasted antpitta as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common in most of its range and occurs in several protected areas.<ref name=SBAN-BOW/>
